panelReasoning: In Chen/Silberschatz ER notation each option maps to a distinct construct - underline = key attribute, double line = total participation, double diamond = the identifying relationship set, double rectangle = the weak entity set - so only D answers the stem as asked. The source key C swaps the identifying relationship for the entity it supports, the classic trap of this item.

The stem is a garbled recall of the canonical IPOS item 'Computers gather data, which means that they allow users to ____ data': gathering data is by definition the intake step, i.e. input, while storing is a separate computer function with its own item in the same series. The keyed 'Store' conflates two of the four functions in the frame, so the panel derives B.

Two judges verified the publisher's own key (SAGE/Banyard 3e ch.5) as D and matched it to Lansdale & Baguley (2008), whose model has the trace population diluted over time by null traces with retrieval organised temporally, so the target loses temporal distinctiveness; B reverses the direction of the null-trace claim and C reads 'dilution' as the similarity-based interference their model replaces. The third judge dissented for C at 0.4 confidence, unable to verify the source paper, so the key stands but is disputed.

Three-way split with no majority: one judge held the item defective because Bartlett (1932) reported all four listed changes, one corroborated the publisher key C from the source HTML at 0.55, and one derived D (intrusions) at 0.6. The skeptic judged the item answerable, so it is not quarantined, but no answer commands two votes and the key cannot be verified - route to a human.

A program that runs in the background and silently reports your browsing habits back to whoever installed it is the textbook definition of spyware; adware is defined by displaying advertisements (none are mentioned) and grayware is only an umbrella term covering spyware and adware, too broad to be the specific category named. The keyed C is the vaguest option, not the best one.

PROLOG (PROgramming in LOGic, Colmerauer 1972) is the canonical AI language of this MCQ family, LISP being the usual alternative and absent here; FORTRAN is numeric, COBOL is business data processing, and C is a general-purpose systems language with no AI-specific constructs. The key 'C' is indefensible and looks like an extraction artifact - the same file keys label C for several consecutive contested items.

The 'traffic cop' metaphor is deciding which way traffic goes at an intersection - path selection between networks - which is the router's job; a hub blindly floods, a modem only modulates, and a switch merely forwards frames by MAC inside one LAN. Published versions of this exact stem answer Router, so the keyed Switch (again label C in this corrupt file) is the weaker reading, though the metaphor is loose enough that all three judges capped confidence at 0.6-0.75.

Printer output quality is resolution, specified in DPI = dots per linear inch (300/600/1200 dpi); 'dots per unit time' is speed (ppm) not quality, which also sinks 'all of above', and 'dot per sq. inch' is a widely copied error in the circulated banks that this source key reproduces. All three judges answer A, while noting a strict grader using the circulated key would mark B.
